children.name: Mus
children.description: The house mouse and its relatives — small, opportunistic omnivores that have followed human civilisation to every continent. Mus musculus is one of the most studied vertebrates on Earth: its genome was sequenced in 2002, and laboratory mice underpin the majority of biomedical research. Wild house mice are extraordinarily adaptable, surviving on Antarctic research stations and in the refrigerated meat lockers of supermarkets at near-freezing temperatures. Their incisors grow continuously and must be worn down by gnawing.

children.name: Mus musculus
children.description: The most abundant mammal on Earth by number of individuals. Domesticated as the 'lab mouse', it has contributed to more Nobel Prizes than any other animal.

children.name: Mus spretus
children.description: A wild relative of the house mouse found around the western Mediterranean. Resistant to certain pesticides that affect house mice, making it a subject of genetic research.

children.name: Rattus
children.description: Rats — medium-sized, highly intelligent rodents with a deeply troubled relationship with humanity. The black rat (Rattus rattus) was the reservoir host for the fleas that spread Yersinia pestis, causing the Black Death. The brown rat (Rattus norvegicus), despite its name, originated in central Asia and reached Europe in the 18th century, largely displacing the black rat. Both species have been implicated in the extinction of numerous island birds and reptiles. Rats also demonstrate empathy: laboratory brown rats will forgo food rewards to release trapped companions.

children.name: Rattus norvegicus
children.description: The dominant commensal rat across most of the world. Lab rats are domesticated brown rats. Wild populations can learn to avoid novel objects (neophobia) and pass this learned behaviour to offspring.

children.name: Rattus rattus
children.description: A superb climber and the original ship rat — spread worldwide by European maritime trade. Responsible for devastating seabird colonies on remote islands; eradication programs have allowed remarkable recoveries.

children.name: Apodemus
children.description: Wood mice and field mice of the Old World — elegant, large-eared mice that inhabit woodland, hedgerow, and field edges across Europe and Asia. The wood mouse (Apodemus sylvaticus) is one of the most abundant wild mammals in Europe and a crucial prey species for owls, foxes, stoats, and weasels. It caches seeds and has a remarkable ability to locate buried food stores it has hidden months earlier. The yellow-necked mouse is larger and bolder, readily entering houses in autumn.

children.name: Apodemus sylvaticus
children.description: One of Europe's most abundant small mammals, forming the base of countless food chains. Can shed its tail skin when seized by a predator, leaving the predator holding only skin while the mouse escapes.

children.name: Apodemus flavicollis
children.description: Identified by its distinctive yellow collar. Larger and more arboreal than the wood mouse, readily climbing high into trees. An important seed disperser in European deciduous woodland.

children.name: Meriones
children.description: Gerbils — social, burrowing rodents of arid and semi-arid regions from North Africa to China. The Mongolian gerbil became a popular laboratory animal and pet in the 1950s; all captive gerbils descend from just 20 individuals caught in Mongolia in 1935. Wild gerbils live in complex multi-chamber burrow systems and are active both day and night. Their kidneys are extraordinarily efficient at concentrating urine — some species can survive on dry seeds alone, producing almost no liquid waste.

children.name: Meriones unguiculatus
children.description: The standard laboratory gerbil. Highly social — solitary individuals develop stereotypic behaviours and show signs of depression. They mark territories with a scent gland on their abdomen.

children.name: Meriones libycus
children.description: A large gerbil of North Africa and the Middle East. Can be a significant agricultural pest, storing large quantities of grain in its burrows. Important prey for desert foxes, eagles, and snakes.

children.name: Mastomys
children.description: Multimammate rats — African rodents named for their extraordinary number of mammary glands (up to 24, versus the usual 10–12 in most rodents). They are among the most prolific breeders of any mammal, capable of producing litters of up to 22 young multiple times per year. Mastomys natalensis is the primary reservoir for Lassa fever virus in West Africa, a viral haemorrhagic fever that infects 100,000–300,000 people annually. Occasional population explosions create rodent plagues that devastate crops across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Mastomys natalensis
children.description: The most abundant rodent in sub-Saharan Africa and one of the most fecund mammals on Earth. Reservoir host of Lassa virus, which it sheds asymptomatically in urine and faeces.
